---
# =====================================================================
#  Research highlight v10 — PI feedback revisions (branches from v9)
#    NOTE ON LINEAGE: v10 branches from v9. v9 in turn branched from v6,
#    not v8 — the version number is sequential but the content is not
#    (v7 and v8 are a separate line of development). v9 took v6's body and
#    restyled it with the v8 design system (maroon section heads); v10
#    carries that forward and applies PI feedback.
#    Structure: Background -> The Challenge -> What the study found
#    (closing on the "in this context" caveat) -> Implications for policy,
#    in two parts — the cost-effectiveness case against compliance
#    assistance, and the case for evaluable program design.
#  Render:  quarto render epa-compliance-workshops_research-highlight_v10.qmd
#  Design system: _style.tex (colours, fonts, boxes, macros)
#  Figure: figures/fig2-event-study.png (built by code/make-fig2-event-study.R)
# =====================================================================
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: pdflatex
    documentclass: article
    fontsize: 11pt
    geometry:
      - top=0.4in
      - bottom=0.45in
      - left=0.65in
      - right=0.65in
      - includehead
      - headheight=52pt
      - headsep=7pt
      - includefoot
      - footskip=24pt
    include-in-header:
      - file: ../../../../formats/research-highlight/_style.tex
      - text: |
          % --- Text-wrap around the Background figure ---
          \usepackage{wrapfig}
          % --- No hyphenation at line breaks (loosen spacing to avoid overruns) ---
          \usepackage[none]{hyphenat}
          \tolerance=9999
          \emergencystretch=3em
          \hbadness=99999
          % --- Running masthead + footer (logo, label, rule, page number) ---
          \usepackage{fancyhdr}
          \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0pt}
          \renewcommand{\footrulewidth}{0pt}
          \fancypagestyle{brief}{%
            \fancyhf{}%
            \fancyhead[L]{%
              \begin{minipage}[c]{0.62\textwidth}\raggedright\color{ink}\includegraphics[height=0.45in]{../../../../formats/logos/eil-logo-maroon.png}\end{minipage}\hfill%
              \begin{minipage}[c]{0.36\textwidth}\raggedleft\fontsize{8.25pt}{10pt}\selectfont\bfseries\color{accentred}\textls[200]{\MakeUppercase{Research Highlight}}\end{minipage}%
              \par\vspace{2pt}%
              \noindent\textcolor{ink}{\rule{\textwidth}{2.2pt}}%
            }%
            \fancyfoot[C]{%
              \color{warmrule}\rule{\textwidth}{0.7pt}\par\vspace{3pt}%
              \hbox to\textwidth{%
                \color{faint}\fontsize{7.4pt}{9pt}\selectfont
                For full paper:\hspace{0.4em}\href{https://doi.org/10.1002/pam.70056}{\color{faint}\underline{\textls[40]{doi.org/10.1002/pam.70056}}}%
                \hfill
                Page \thepage%
              }%
            }%
          }
          \pagestyle{brief}
    keep-tex: true
---

```{=latex}
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% TITLE + CITATION  (masthead repeats in the page header)
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\brieftitle{No evidence that compliance assistance reduces environmental violations}
\briefcitation{Lessons from ``The Effect of Compliance Assistance on Pollution Discharges and Violations of Environmental Regulations'' by Paul J. Ferraro \& Jay P. Shimshack in \textit{Journal of Policy Analysis and Management}, 2026.}

\vspace{-2pt}

% override body font size for this document
\fontsize{10.5pt}{13.5pt}\selectfont

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% BOTTOM LINE
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{bottomline}
{\fontsize{11pt}{15pt}\selectfont
In new research studying hundreds of polluting facilities that were offered free compliance assistance workshops and training, \textbf{offering polluting facilities free help to follow the rules didn't make them more likely to follow them.}
}
\end{bottomline}

\vspace{6pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% BACKGROUND  (broad — poses the question)
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\sectionhead{Background}
\begin{wrapfigure}{r}{3.1in}
  \vspace{-\intextsep}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=3.1in]{../../data-viz/figures/fig-two-approaches.pdf}
\end{wrapfigure}
\noindent Environmental enforcement in the U.S. has long centered on inspecting facilities, catching violations, and levying fines. \textbf{Compliance assistance} takes a different approach: regulators offer workshops, technical guidance, and other support to help facilities understand and meet regulatory requirements. This approach reflects the belief that many violations result from confusion or limited expertise rather than deliberate corner-cutting. Compliance assistance programs have become increasingly popular as a policy lever, but evidence on whether they are effective is scarce.

\noindent \textbf{In a new study, Ferraro and Shimshack (2026) find that compliance assistance workshops and training did not reduce regulatory violations or pollution discharges.} The researchers analyzed data from hundreds of Ohio wastewater treatment facilities that had recently been cited for violations and were invited to attend workshops rolled out over several months.

\vspace{6pt}
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% HOW THE STUDY TESTED IT  (narrowing — setting + research design)
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\sectionhead{The Challenge}
\noindent \textbf{Evaluating whether compliance assistance programs are effective is difficult to do in practice.} Simply comparing the behavior of facilities before vs. after receiving the training risks attributing program success to changes in behavior that are actually driven by other factors.

\noindent Comparing facilities that signed up against those that never did also raises some concerns. The facilities that sign up for a workshop might be different from those that don't in ways that have nothing to do with the workshop, like a manager who cares about reducing violations. Comparing attendees and non-attendees might simply reflect who chose to walk through the door, not what they learned once inside.

\noindent To avoid these concerns, \textbf{Ferraro and Shimshack compared facilities that received compliance assistance and training early to facilities that had signed up but not yet received the training.} Arguably, these facilities would have had similar motivations and similar histories. The absence of differences between these facilities prior to receiving the training provides support for this.

\vspace{6pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% WHAT THE STUDY FOUND  (the waist — result, precision, discharges)
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\sectionhead{No effect on violations or discharges}
\noindent Compared with not-yet-trained facilities, trained facilities violated environmental regulations just as often and discharged just as much pollution. That held within facilities as well as across them: tracking the same facilities over time, their violations did not decline in the months after they attended a workshop (see figure). Ferraro and Shimshack find no support for the expectation that compliance assistance and training reduce violations or pollution discharges. \textbf{At least in this context, a lack of understanding at these facilities does not appear to be what was holding compliance back.}

\vspace{6pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% FIGURE — event study: effect on violations by month around training
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{center}
\begin{minipage}{0.75\linewidth}
\figtitle{The workshops' effect on violations, month by month}{Source: Ferraro \& Shimshack (2026), Fig.\ 2a.}
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{../../data-viz/figures/fig2-event-study.png}
\par\vspace{3pt}
{\color{faint}\fontsize{7.1pt}{9.5pt}\selectfont \textit{Note:} This figure shows the estimated effect of compliance assistance workshops on the number of facility violations. Each dot shows whether facilities had more (above the zero line) or fewer (below the zero line) violations in a given month relative to the workshop month (vertical dashed line). The estimates account for each facility's usual number of violations, the local weather, and any common trends shared across facilities; the shaded band shows the range of estimates within the 95\% confidence interval. If the workshops had an effect on violations, the line would drop below zero after the workshop.\par}
\end{minipage}
\end{center}

\vspace{6pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% POLICY IMPLICATIONS  (thinking bigger picture about the findings)
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\sectionhead{Implications for policy}
\noindent If these results hold in other contexts -- beyond Ohio wastewater treatment plants --  they strongly caution against leaning on compliance assistance to reduce violations and pollution. The evidence that inspections and penalties work is stronger. With enforcement budgets already shrinking, \textbf{funding a compliance assistance program out of that same budget could mean more violations and more pollution}.

\noindent One program that didn't work isn't a verdict on compliance assistance everywhere. Rather, \textbf{this study highlights the value of rigorous program evaluation}. It also points to an opportunity for researchers and regulators to work together: by piloting new programs and measuring their effects before rolling them out widely, researchers can help policymakers learn what works, advancing evidence-based policy making.

\vspace{6pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% FOR MORE INFO
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{moreinfo}
Contact Jay Shimshack (jay.shimshack@virginia.edu) with questions about this research.\par

Ferraro, Paul J., and Jay P. Shimshack. 2026. ``The Effect of Compliance Assistance on Pollution Discharges and Violations of Environmental Regulations.'' \textit{Journal of Policy Analysis and Management} 45 (3): e70056. \href{https://doi.org/10.1002/pam.70056}{\color{cite}\underline{doi.org/10.1002/pam.70056}}.\par

\textbf{Paul J. Ferraro} is the Bloomberg Distinguished Professor of Human Behavior and Public Policy at Johns Hopkins University.\par
\textbf{Jay P. Shimshack} is a Professor of Public Policy and Economics at the University of Virginia's Batten School.\par

The \href{https://www.environmental-inequality-lab.org/}{\color{cite}\underline{Environmental Inequality Lab}} is a nonpartisan research group that applies a rigorous, data-driven approach to understand how our environment shapes economic opportunity and well-being.\par
\end{moreinfo}

```
